title: John Swinney would 'consider' Prestwick Airport ban for US military planes

sections.heading: What happened
sections.content: US military aircraft could be banned from using Prestwick Airport by First Minister John Swinney if it was confirmed they were involved in strikes in the Middle East. This comes after Swinney said he was "concerned" by the US and Israeli action against Iran. Speaking to the BBC's Laura Kuenssberg , Swinney noted he was seeking clarity from the UK Government about US planes using the Scottish Government -owned airport. He said: "We're seeking clarity from the United Kingdom Government about the purpose of American flights that are coming in and out of Prestwick, but we don't have the ability to scrutinise those flights."

sections.heading: Key details
sections.content: The First Minister said he was seeking clarity from the UK government on the purpose of American aircraft going through Prestwick to the Middle East. When asked if he would ban them if confirmed that they were involved with the Middle East operation, Swinney said: "I will consider that based on the evidence that's provided to me." Prestwick Airport has seen hundreds of US military flights using the airport in recent years. The Scottish Greens said 24 US military planes had landed at the airport in the week prior to the action against Iran during First Minister's Questions on Thursday.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.content: Co-leader Ross Greer accused Swinney of "sitting on his hands" and urged him to follow the example of Spain by banning them. It was "abundantly obvious" what the latest US flights were being used for, Greer said. In November 2023, an Israeli air force plane refuelled at Prestwick Airport, which resulted in similar questions being thrown the way of the Scottish Government.
